content.full_text:
March 6, 1980

Mr. Harold Taylor
202 Avenida Santa Margarita
San Clemente, California 92672

Dear Mr. Taylor:

In your letter of February 11, 1980, you ask whether an advertisement in the Daily Sun Post, a copy of which you enclosed, is a "political advertisement."

The response we give to this question is limited to the requirements of the California Political Reform Act (Govt. Code §§81000-91014), the law we administer.

Generally speaking, the Political Reform Act requires the reporting and disclosure of payments that the Act defines as "expenditures" (see Govt. Code §82025 and 2 Cal. Admin. Code §18225).

All payments made by organizations formed or existing primarily for political purposes are defined to be expenditures (see 2 Cal. Admin. Code §18225(b)(4)).

For other organizations payments are expenditures if they are used for communications which expressly advocate a nomination, election or defeat of a clearly identified candidate or candidates (see 2 Cal. Admin. Code §18225(c)).

A candidate is "clearly identified" if the communication states his name, makes unambiguous reference to his office or status as a candidate, or unambiguously describes him in any manner (see 2 Cal. Admin. Code §18225(c)(1)).

A communication "expressly advocates" the nomination, election or defeat of a candidate if it contains express words of advocacy such as "vote for," "elect," "support," "cast your ballot," "vote against," "defeat," "reject," "sign petitions for" or otherwise refers to a clearly identified candidate so that the communication, taken as a whole, unambiguously urges a particular result in an election (see 2 Cal. Admin. Code §18225(c)(2)).

- Mr. Harold Taylor                          -2-                          March 6, 1930


     To the extent that the advertisement you have supplied us
meets the above criteria, it may produce reporting and disclosure
obligations under the Political Reform Act. Such a determination
could not be made, however, without additional information about
whether the person placing the advertisement is a committee, the
cost of the advertisement, and whether it was placed at the behest
of the candidates it supports or their committees.

     I hope this response is helpful to you. If you desire
additional assistance, you may call me at 916/322-5662.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        John Giansello
                                        Political Reform Analyst

JG:bw

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
107 SOUTH BROADWAY • • • SUITE 7007 • • • LOS ANGELES, 90012

March 6, 1980


Mr. Lee Hendrie
9582 Zetland
Huntington Beach, California 92646

Dear Mr. Hendrie:

     This letter is to confirm my advice to you last February
29, 1980 during our telephone conversation.

     The situation you described is as follows: an individual,
other than the candidate, made payments in behalf of the campaign.
The candidate's controlled committee, of which you are the
treasurer, later reimbursed the individual for those payments.
You specifically asked whether or not the payments originally
made by the individual directly to the vendors in behalf of
the campaign would have to be reported as contributions even
though the campaign reported on Schedule E, Payments and
Contributions Made, the payment(s) to the individual reimbursing
them for their campaign expenditures?

     Government Code Section 82025 defines a reportable
expenditure as "a payment, a forgiveness of a loan, a payment
of a loan by a third party, or an enforceable promise to make
a payment, unless it is clear from the surrounding circumstances
that it is not made for political purposes. An expenditure is
made on the date the payment is made or on the date consideration,
if any, is received, whichever is earlier."

     In addition, Government Code Section 82015, in part, defines
a reportable contribution as any payment or "expenditure made at
the behest of a candidate, committee or elected officeholder..."
A contribution does not "include volunteer personal services or
payments made by an individual for his own travel expenses if such
payments are made voluntarily without any understanding or
agreement that they shall be, directly or indirectly, repaid to
him."

     Therefore, the payments made by the individual at the behest of
the campaign are to be reported as Non-monetary Contributions
on Schedule C as well as on Schedule E as reimbursements to the
individual.


SACRAMENTO (916) 322-5660  •  LOS ANGELES (213) 620-5196

Mr. Lee Hendrick
March 6, 1980
Page 2

     I hope this information has been helpful to you, if
I can be of any further assistance, please do not hesitate
to contact me.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Helen P. Arriola
                                        Political Reform
                                        Consultant

HPA:jg

RECEIVED
FEB 13 1980
Enforcement, FPPC
Los Angeles

FPPC
FEB 19 2 30 PM '80

February 11, 1980
202 Avenida Santa Margarita
San Clemente, California 92672

Fair Political Practices Commission
107 South Broadway, Room 7007
Los Angeles, California 90012

Dear Sir:

Is the enclosed paid advertisement that appeared
in the Daily Sun Post, our local newspaper, a political
advertisement?

Please send me an opinion on this matter.

Thank you.

Yours truly,

Harold Taylor
Harold Taylor

RESOLUTION

WHEREAS, Mayor William H. Walker and Councilwoman Donna Wilkinson
have faithfully and diligently given a combined total of twenty-five
years of dedicated service for the betterment and welfare of the City
of San Clemente;

WHEREAS, they have a proven record of competence and efficient
administration as members of the City Parks and Recreation Commission,
the Traffic and Parking Commission, the Planning Commission and the
City Council;

WHEREAS, during the discharge of their duties and responsibilities
in their various official capacities they have proven their financial
responsibility in regards to the city's budget including the financing
and establishment of a Corporate Park and a Downtown Parking District;

WHEREAS, they have supported improvements in the city's beautifi-
cation and parks including downtown beautification and the development
of the North Beach Parking lot, Mira Costa Park, Linda Lane Park, San
Clemente Park and rebuilding of the Pier;

WHEREAS, they have supported recreation activities including
founding of the city volleyball tournament, the Cocoa Festival, the
Fiesta Parade and Carnival, Fourth of July activities and the Chlotilda
Pageant;

WHEREAS, they have supported development of community services
and education including the Police Explorer Post, the Rape Crisis Center,
the Boys and Girls Gymnasium, the Fire Explorer Post, and Regional
Occupation training by the Fire Department;

WHEREAS, they have worked for and supported development of a
healthy business climate in the City of San Clemente;

WHEREAS, their support of these items is a matter of public record;

THEREFORE, BE IT RESOLVED BY THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS OF THE CHAMBER
OF COMMERCE OF SAN CLEMENTE, that the Board of Directors of the Chamber
of Commerce of San Clemente recommends to the members of the Chamber
and to all voters of the City of San Clemente that they vote in the
Special Election Tuesday, January 23, 1979, not to recall Mayor William
H. Walker and Councilwoman Donna Wilkinson.

[signatures]
Alex W. Goodman          Bernard G. Allen
Secretary                President

January 9, 1979

(PAID FOR BY THE SAN CLEMENTE CHAMBER OF COMMERCE)
